{{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Musashi Fuchū Kumano Jinja Kofun'''|武蔵府中熊野神社古墳 |}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]] located in the city of [[:en:Fuchū, Tokyo|Fuchū, Tokyo]] in the [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It received protection as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1970.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/192672 |title=武蔵府中熊野神社古墳|trans-title= Musashi Fuchū Kumano Jinja Kofun |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Musashi Fuchu Kumano Jinja Kofun is located on the north side of a [[Kumano shrine]] in Fuchū City. The tumulus was believed for centuries to be a natural hill, although there was a local tradition that it contained a cave. The shrine itself was relocated to this site in 1777 and has no connection with the tumulus. In 1990, the shrine decided to remove part of the tumulus to make room for the construction of a new storage shed for its ''[[mikoshi]]'' floats, and the hill was discovered to be of artificial construction made of rammed earth and river stones. However, as the location is far from any known ''kofun'' tumuli clusters, even at this time it was not recognized as being a ''kofun''. In 1994, further investigation using [[:en:Ground penetrating radar|Ground penetrating radar]] indicated the presence of a large stone structure near the top of the mound, raising the possibility that this was indeed a burial tumulus. Shortly afterwards, a local historical journal article dated August 1884 was discovered, stating that a [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] had been found within the mound, and that this chamber was divided into three rooms. The innermost room contained two human skeletons, and artifacts resembling "rusty nails". A complete archaeological excavation was conducted from May 2003, in which the hill was confirmed to be a three-tier {{nihongo|{{ill|Joenkahofun|lt=Joenkahofun|ja|上円下方墳|1=Joenkahofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joenkahofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Joenkahofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}|上円下方墳|1=Joenkahofun}}, with two square base tiers and an upper dome. It is the third of this style of tumulus to have been discovered in Japan, and is one of the largest ''kofun'' in the [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi area]]. The bottom tier is 32 meters long on each side, the middle tier is 23 meters long on each side, and the dome has a diameter of 16 meters and a height of 2.5 meters. The burial chamber had a length of 8.8 meters. Traces of a moat with a width of six meters and depth of two meters have been found on the east and south sides of the tumulus. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
As the tomb had been opened in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], and possibly earlier, there were few [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]]; however, artifacts recovered included one silver inlaid sheath with metal fittings, six glass beads, three iron swords, and about 300 iron nails. From these artifacts, the tumulus was built from the middle to the latter half of the 7th century AD. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
In 2005 it was designated as a National Historic Site. Fuchū city has been active in promoting the site as a tourist attraction, and has restored the mound with ''[[fukiishi]]'' to what is believed to be its original appearance, and opening an adjacent museum, the {{nihongo|'''Fuchū Kyodo no Mori Museum'''|府中市郷土の森博物館|}}. It is located about 8 minutes on foot from [[:en:Nishifu Station|Nishifu Station]] on the [[:en:East Japan Railway Company|East Japan Railway Company]][[:en:Nambu Line|Nambu Line]]. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> 武蔵府中熊野神社古墳出土 鞘尻金具 (複製).JPG|Scabbard metal fittings found within the Musashi Fuchū Kumano Jinja Kofun 武蔵府中熊野神社古墳 出土品.JPG|Beads and sword hilt from the Musashi Fuchū Kumano Jinja Kofun 武蔵府中熊野神社古墳出土 釘-1.JPG|Iron nails from the Musashi Fuchū Kumano Jinja Kofun </gallery>

{{ill|Joenkahofun|ja|上円下方墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joenkahofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} | name = Musashi Fuchū Kumano Jinja Kofun | native_name =武蔵府中熊野神社古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = File:Musashi-Fuchu Kumano-jinja Kofun, funkyu-1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption =Musashi Fuchū Kumano Jinja Kofun | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Musashi Fuchū Kumano Jinja Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.675139|N|139.457083|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = {{ill|Fuchū|WD=Q208818}} | region = {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region}} | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = late 7th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Musashi Fuchū Kumano Jinja Kofun'''|武蔵府中熊野神社古墳 |}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]] located in the city of {{ill|Fuchū, Tokyo|en|Fuchū, Tokyo|WD=Q208818|lt=Fuchū, Tokyo}} in the {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. It received protection as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1970.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/192672 |title=武蔵府中熊野神社古墳|trans-title= Musashi Fuchū Kumano Jinja Kofun |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Musashi Fuchu Kumano Jinja Kofun is located on the north side of a [[Kumano shrine]] in Fuchū City. The tumulus was believed for centuries to be a natural hill, although there was a local tradition that it contained a cave. The shrine itself was relocated to this site in 1777 and has no connection with the tumulus. In 1990, the shrine decided to remove part of the tumulus to make room for the construction of a new storage shed for its ''[[mikoshi]]'' floats, and the hill was discovered to be of artificial construction made of rammed earth and river stones. However, as the location is far from any known ''kofun'' tumuli clusters, even at this time it was not recognized as being a ''kofun''. In 1994, further investigation using [[:en:Ground penetrating radar|Ground penetrating radar]] indicated the presence of a large stone structure near the top of the mound, raising the possibility that this was indeed a burial tumulus. Shortly afterwards, a local historical journal article dated August 1884 was discovered, stating that a [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] had been found within the mound, and that this chamber was divided into three rooms. The innermost room contained two human skeletons, and artifacts resembling "rusty nails". A complete archaeological excavation was conducted from May 2003, in which the hill was confirmed to be a three-tier {{nihongo|{{ill|Joenkahofun|lt=Joenkahofun|ja|上円下方墳|1=Joenkahofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joenkahofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Joenkahofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}|上円下方墳|1=Joenkahofun}}, with two square base tiers and an upper dome. It is the third of this style of tumulus to have been discovered in Japan, and is one of the largest ''kofun'' in the {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi area}}. The bottom tier is 32 meters long on each side, the middle tier is 23 meters long on each side, and the dome has a diameter of 16 meters and a height of 2.5 meters. The burial chamber had a length of 8.8 meters. Traces of a moat with a width of six meters and depth of two meters have been found on the east and south sides of the tumulus. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
As the tomb had been opened in the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, and possibly earlier, there were few {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods}}; however, artifacts recovered included one silver inlaid sheath with metal fittings, six glass beads, three iron swords, and about 300 iron nails. From these artifacts, the tumulus was built from the middle to the latter half of the 7th century AD. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
In 2005 it was designated as a National Historic Site. Fuchū city has been active in promoting the site as a tourist attraction, and has restored the mound with ''[[fukiishi]]'' to what is believed to be its original appearance, and opening an adjacent museum, the {{nihongo|'''Fuchū Kyodo no Mori Museum'''|府中市郷土の森博物館|}}. It is located about 8 minutes on foot from {{ill|Nishifu Station|en|Nishifu Station|WD=Q5366323|lt=Nishifu Station}} on the {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|WD=Q499071|lt=East Japan Railway Company}}{{ill|Nambu Line|en|Nambu Line|WD=Q1366263|lt=Nambu Line}}. <ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Joenkahofun|ja|上円下方墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joenkahofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} | name = Musashi Fuchū Kumano Jinja Kofun | native_name =武蔵府中熊野神社古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = File:Musashi-Fuchu Kumano-jinja Kofun, funkyu-1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption =Musashi Fuchū Kumano Jinja Kofun | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Musashi Fuchū Kumano Jinja Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.675139|N|139.457083|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = {{ill|Fuchū|WD=Q208818}} | region = {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region}} | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = late 7th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles ={{ill|round-topped burial mound|WD=Q11358496}} | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Musashi Fuchū Kumano Jinja Kofun'''|武蔵府中熊野神社古墳 |}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]] located in the city of {{ill|Fuchū, Tokyo|en|Fuchū, Tokyo|WD=Q208818|lt=Fuchū, Tokyo}} in the {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. It received protection as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1970.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/192672 |title=武蔵府中熊野神社古墳|trans-title= Musashi Fuchū Kumano Jinja Kofun |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Musashi Fuchu Kumano Jinja Kofun is located on the north side of a [[Kumano shrine]] in Fuchū City. The tumulus was believed for centuries to be a natural hill, although there was a local tradition that it contained a cave. The shrine itself was relocated to this site in 1777 and has no connection with the tumulus. In 1990, the shrine decided to remove part of the tumulus to make room for the construction of a new storage shed for its ''[[mikoshi]]'' floats, and the hill was discovered to be of artificial construction made of rammed earth and river stones. However, as the location is far from any known ''kofun'' tumuli clusters, even at this time it was not recognized as being a ''kofun''. In 1994, further investigation using [[:en:Ground penetrating radar|Ground penetrating radar]] indicated the presence of a large stone structure near the top of the mound, raising the possibility that this was indeed a burial tumulus. Shortly afterwards, a local historical journal article dated August 1884 was discovered, stating that a [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] had been found within the mound, and that this chamber was divided into three rooms. The innermost room contained two human skeletons, and artifacts resembling "rusty nails". A complete archaeological excavation was conducted from May 2003, in which the hill was confirmed to be a three-tier {{nihongo|{{ill|Joenkahofun|lt=Joenkahofun|ja|上円下方墳|1=Joenkahofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joenkahofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Joenkahofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}|上円下方墳|1=Joenkahofun}}, with two square base tiers and an upper dome. It is the third of this style of tumulus to have been discovered in Japan, and is one of the largest ''kofun'' in the {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi area}}. The bottom tier is 32 meters long on each side, the middle tier is 23 meters long on each side, and the dome has a diameter of 16 meters and a height of 2.5 meters. The burial chamber had a length of 8.8 meters. Traces of a moat with a width of six meters and depth of two meters have been found on the east and south sides of the tumulus. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
As the tomb had been opened in the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, and possibly earlier, there were few {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods}}; however, artifacts recovered included one silver inlaid sheath with metal fittings, six glass beads, three iron swords, and about 300 iron nails. From these artifacts, the tumulus was built from the middle to the latter half of the 7th century AD. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
In 2005 it was designated as a National Historic Site. Fuchū city has been active in promoting the site as a tourist attraction, and has restored the mound with ''[[fukiishi]]'' to what is believed to be its original appearance, and opening an adjacent museum, the {{nihongo|'''Fuchū Kyodo no Mori Museum'''|府中市郷土の森博物館|}}. It is located about 8 minutes on foot from {{ill|Nishifu Station|en|Nishifu Station|WD=Q5366323|lt=Nishifu Station}} on the {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|WD=Q499071|lt=East Japan Railway Company}}{{ill|Nambu Line|en|Nambu Line|WD=Q1366263|lt=Nambu Line}}. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> 武蔵府中熊野神社古墳出土 鞘尻金具 (複製).JPG|Scabbard metal fittings found within the Musashi Fuchū Kumano Jinja Kofun 武蔵府中熊野神社古墳 出土品.JPG|Beads and sword hilt from the Musashi Fuchū Kumano Jinja Kofun 武蔵府中熊野神社古墳出土 釘-1.JPG|Iron nails from the Musashi Fuchū Kumano Jinja Kofun </gallery>
==See also== {{Portal|History|Japan}} *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Tōkyō)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Tōkyō)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons category-inline}} * [https://www.city.fuchu.tokyo.jp/kirari/sagasu/theme/muryou/kumanoginzyakohun.html Fuchū city official home page] {{in lang|ja}} * [https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=99 Tokyo Cultural properties Database] {{in lang|ja}}

{{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Joenkahofun|ja|上円下方墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joenkahofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} | name = Musashi Fuchū Kumano Jinja Kofun | native_name =武蔵府中熊野神社古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = File:Musashi-Fuchu Kumano-jinja Kofun, funkyu-1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption =Musashi Fuchū Kumano Jinja Kofun | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Musashi Fuchū Kumano Jinja Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.675139|N|139.457083|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = {{ill|Fuchū|WD=Q208818|1=Fuchū, Tokyo}} | region = {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region|1=Kantō region}} | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = late 7th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles ={{ill|round-topped burial mound|WD=Q11358496|1=Joenkahofun}} | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Musashi Fuchū Kumano Jinja Kofun'''|武蔵府中熊野神社古墳 |}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]] located in the city of {{ill|Fuchū, Tokyo|en|Fuchū, Tokyo|WD=Q208818|lt=Fuchū, Tokyo|1=Fuchū, Tokyo}} in the {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region|1=Kantō region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. It received protection as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1970.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/192672 |title=武蔵府中熊野神社古墳|trans-title= Musashi Fuchū Kumano Jinja Kofun |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Musashi Fuchu Kumano Jinja Kofun is located on the north side of a [[Kumano shrine]] in Fuchū City. The tumulus was believed for centuries to be a natural hill, although there was a local tradition that it contained a cave. The shrine itself was relocated to this site in 1777 and has no connection with the tumulus. In 1990, the shrine decided to remove part of the tumulus to make room for the construction of a new storage shed for its ''[[mikoshi]]'' floats, and the hill was discovered to be of artificial construction made of rammed earth and river stones. However, as the location is far from any known ''kofun'' tumuli clusters, even at this time it was not recognized as being a ''kofun''. In 1994, further investigation using [[:en:Ground penetrating radar|Ground penetrating radar]] indicated the presence of a large stone structure near the top of the mound, raising the possibility that this was indeed a burial tumulus. Shortly afterwards, a local historical journal article dated August 1884 was discovered, stating that a [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] had been found within the mound, and that this chamber was divided into three rooms. The innermost room contained two human skeletons, and artifacts resembling "rusty nails". A complete archaeological excavation was conducted from May 2003, in which the hill was confirmed to be a three-tier {{nihongo|{{ill|Joenkahofun|lt=Joenkahofun|ja|上円下方墳|1=Joenkahofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joenkahofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Joenkahofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}|上円下方墳|1=Joenkahofun}}, with two square base tiers and an upper dome. It is the third of this style of tumulus to have been discovered in Japan, and is one of the largest ''kofun'' in the {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi area|1=Musashi Province}}. The bottom tier is 32 meters long on each side, the middle tier is 23 meters long on each side, and the dome has a diameter of 16 meters and a height of 2.5 meters. The burial chamber had a length of 8.8 meters. Traces of a moat with a width of six meters and depth of two meters have been found on the east and south sides of the tumulus. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
As the tomb had been opened in the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, and possibly earlier, there were few {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods|1=Grave goods}}; however, artifacts recovered included one silver inlaid sheath with metal fittings, six glass beads, three iron swords, and about 300 iron nails. From these artifacts, the tumulus was built from the middle to the latter half of the 7th century AD. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
In 2005 it was designated as a National Historic Site. Fuchū city has been active in promoting the site as a tourist attraction, and has restored the mound with ''[[fukiishi]]'' to what is believed to be its original appearance, and opening an adjacent museum, the {{nihongo|'''Fuchū Kyodo no Mori Museum'''|府中市郷土の森博物館|}}. It is located about 8 minutes on foot from {{ill|Nishifu Station|en|Nishifu Station|WD=Q5366323|lt=Nishifu Station|1=Nishifu Station}} on the {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|WD=Q499071|lt=East Japan Railway Company|1=East Japan Railway Company}}{{ill|Nambu Line|en|Nambu Line|WD=Q1366263|lt=Nambu Line|1=Nambu Line}}. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> 武蔵府中熊野神社古墳出土 鞘尻金具 (複製).JPG|Scabbard metal fittings found within the Musashi Fuchū Kumano Jinja Kofun 武蔵府中熊野神社古墳 出土品.JPG|Beads and sword hilt from the Musashi Fuchū Kumano Jinja Kofun 武蔵府中熊野神社古墳出土 釘-1.JPG|Iron nails from the Musashi Fuchū Kumano Jinja Kofun </gallery>

{{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Joenkahofun|ja|上円下方墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joenkahofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} | name = Musashi Fuchū Kumano Jinja Kofun | native_name =武蔵府中熊野神社古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = File:Musashi-Fuchu Kumano-jinja Kofun, funkyu-1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption =Musashi Fuchū Kumano Jinja Kofun | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Musashi Fuchū Kumano Jinja Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.675139|N|139.457083|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = {{ill|Fuchū|qid=Q208818|1=Fuchū, Tokyo}} | region = {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|qid=Q132480|lt=Kantō region|1=Kantō region}} | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = late 7th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles ={{ill|round-topped burial mound|qid=Q11358496|1=Joenkahofun}} | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Musashi Fuchū Kumano Jinja Kofun'''|武蔵府中熊野神社古墳 |}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]] located in the city of {{ill|Fuchū, Tokyo|en|Fuchū, Tokyo|qid=Q208818|lt=Fuchū, Tokyo|1=Fuchū, Tokyo}} in the {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|qid=Q132480|lt=Kantō region|1=Kantō region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. It received protection as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1970.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/192672 |title=武蔵府中熊野神社古墳|trans-title= Musashi Fuchū Kumano Jinja Kofun |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Musashi Fuchu Kumano Jinja Kofun is located on the north side of a [[Kumano shrine]] in Fuchū City. The tumulus was believed for centuries to be a natural hill, although there was a local tradition that it contained a cave. The shrine itself was relocated to this site in 1777 and has no connection with the tumulus. In 1990, the shrine decided to remove part of the tumulus to make room for the construction of a new storage shed for its ''[[mikoshi]]'' floats, and the hill was discovered to be of artificial construction made of rammed earth and river stones. However, as the location is far from any known ''kofun'' tumuli clusters, even at this time it was not recognized as being a ''kofun''. In 1994, further investigation using [[:en:Ground penetrating radar|Ground penetrating radar]] indicated the presence of a large stone structure near the top of the mound, raising the possibility that this was indeed a burial tumulus. Shortly afterwards, a local historical journal article dated August 1884 was discovered, stating that a [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] had been found within the mound, and that this chamber was divided into three rooms. The innermost room contained two human skeletons, and artifacts resembling "rusty nails". A complete archaeological excavation was conducted from May 2003, in which the hill was confirmed to be a three-tier {{nihongo|{{ill|Joenkahofun|lt=Joenkahofun|ja|上円下方墳|1=Joenkahofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joenkahofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Joenkahofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}|上円下方墳|1=Joenkahofun}}, with two square base tiers and an upper dome. It is the third of this style of tumulus to have been discovered in Japan, and is one of the largest ''kofun'' in the {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|qid=Q907398|lt=Musashi area|1=Musashi Province}}. The bottom tier is 32 meters long on each side, the middle tier is 23 meters long on each side, and the dome has a diameter of 16 meters and a height of 2.5 meters. The burial chamber had a length of 8.8 meters. Traces of a moat with a width of six meters and depth of two meters have been found on the east and south sides of the tumulus. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
As the tomb had been opened in the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, and possibly earlier, there were few {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|qid=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods|1=Grave goods}}; however, artifacts recovered included one silver inlaid sheath with metal fittings, six glass beads, three iron swords, and about 300 iron nails. From these artifacts, the tumulus was built from the middle to the latter half of the 7th century AD. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
In 2005 it was designated as a National Historic Site. Fuchū city has been active in promoting the site as a tourist attraction, and has restored the mound with ''[[fukiishi]]'' to what is believed to be its original appearance, and opening an adjacent museum, the {{nihongo|'''Fuchū Kyodo no Mori Museum'''|府中市郷土の森博物館|}}. It is located about 8 minutes on foot from {{ill|Nishifu Station|en|Nishifu Station|qid=Q5366323|lt=Nishifu Station|1=Nishifu Station}} on the {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|qid=Q499071|lt=East Japan Railway Company|1=East Japan Railway Company}}{{ill|Nambu Line|en|Nambu Line|qid=Q1366263|lt=Nambu Line|1=Nambu Line}}. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> 武蔵府中熊野神社古墳出土 鞘尻金具 (複製).JPG|Scabbard metal fittings found within the Musashi Fuchū Kumano Jinja Kofun 武蔵府中熊野神社古墳 出土品.JPG|Beads and sword hilt from the Musashi Fuchū Kumano Jinja Kofun 武蔵府中熊野神社古墳出土 釘-1.JPG|Iron nails from the Musashi Fuchū Kumano Jinja Kofun </gallery>
